semantic_type.name: Organism
semantic_type.definition: Generally, a living individual, including all plants and animals.

semantic_type.name: Plant
semantic_type.definition: An organism having cellulose cell walls, growing by synthesis of inorganic substances, generally distinguished by the presence of chlorophyll, and lacking the power of locomotion. Plant parts are included here as well.

semantic_type.name: Fungus
semantic_type.definition: A eukaryotic organism characterized by the absence of chlorophyll and the presence of a rigid cell wall. Included here are both slime molds and true fungi such as yeasts, molds, mildews, and mushrooms.

semantic_type.name: Virus
semantic_type.definition: An organism consisting of a core of a single nucleic acid enclosed in a protective coat of protein. A virus may replicate only inside a host living cell. A virus exhibits some but not all of the usual characteristics of living things.

semantic_type.name: Bacterium
semantic_type.definition: A small, typically one-celled, prokaryotic micro-organism.

semantic_type.name: Animal
semantic_type.definition: An organism with eukaryotic cells, and lacking stiff cell walls, plastids and photosynthetic pigments.

semantic_type.name: Vertebrate
semantic_type.definition: An animal which has a spinal column.

semantic_type.name: Amphibian
semantic_type.definition: A cold-blooded, smooth-skinned vertebrate which characteristically hatches as an aquatic larva, breathing by gills. When mature, the amphibian breathes with lungs.

semantic_type.name: Bird
semantic_type.definition: A vertebrate having a constant body temperature and characterized by the presence of feathers.

semantic_type.name: Fish
semantic_type.definition: A cold-blooded aquatic vertebrate characterized by fins and breathing by gills. Included here are fishes having either a bony skeleton, such as a perch, or a cartilaginous skeleton, such as a shark, or those lacking a jaw, such as a lamprey or hagfish.

semantic_type.name: Reptile
semantic_type.definition: A cold-blooded vertebrate having an external covering of scales or horny plates. Reptiles breathe by means of lungs and are generally egg-laying.

semantic_type.name: Mammal
semantic_type.definition: A vertebrate having a constant body temperature and characterized by the presence of hair, mammary glands and sweat glands.

semantic_type.name: Human
semantic_type.definition: Modern man, the only remaining species of the Homo genus.

semantic_type.name: Anatomical Structure
semantic_type.definition: A normal or pathological part of the anatomy or structural organization of an organism.

semantic_type.name: Embryonic Structure
semantic_type.definition: An anatomical structure that exists only before the organism is fully formed; in mammals, for example, a structure that exists only prior to the birth of the organism. This structure may be normal or abnormal.

semantic_type.name: Congenital Abnormality
semantic_type.definition: An abnormal structure, or one that is abnormal in size or location, present at birth or evolving over time as a result of a defect in embryogenesis.

semantic_type.name: Acquired Abnormality
semantic_type.definition: An abnormal structure, or one that is abnormal in size or location, found in or deriving from a previously normal structure. Acquired abnormalities are distinguished from diseases even though they may result in pathological functioning (e.g., "hernias incarcerate").

semantic_type.name: Fully Formed Anatomical Structure
semantic_type.definition: An anatomical structure in a fully formed organism; in mammals, for example, a structure in the body after the birth of the organism.

semantic_type.name: Body System
semantic_type.definition: A complex of anatomical structures that performs a common function.

semantic_type.name: Body Part, Organ, or Organ Component
semantic_type.definition: A collection of cells and tissues which are localized to a specific area or combine and carry out one or more specialized functions of an organism. This ranges from gross structures to small components of complex organs. These structures are relatively localized in comparison to tissues.

semantic_type.name: Tissue
semantic_type.definition: An aggregation of similarly specialized cells and the associated intercellular substance. Tissues are relatively non-localized in comparison to body parts, organs or organ components.

semantic_type.name: Cell
semantic_type.definition: The fundamental structural and functional unit of living organisms.

semantic_type.name: Cell Component
semantic_type.definition: A part of a cell or the intercellular matrix, generally visible by light microscopy.

semantic_type.name: Gene or Genome
semantic_type.definition: A specific sequence, or in the case of the genome the complete sequence, of nucleotides along a molecule of DNA or RNA (in the case of some viruses) which represent the functional units of heredity.

semantic_type.name: Body Location or Region
semantic_type.definition: An area, subdivision, or region of the body demarcated for the purpose of topographical description.

semantic_type.name: Body Space or Junction
semantic_type.definition: An area enclosed or surrounded by body parts or organs or the place where two anatomical structures meet or connect.

semantic_type.name: Body Substance
semantic_type.definition: Extracellular material, or mixtures of cells and extracellular material, produced, excreted, or accreted by the body. Included here are substances such as saliva, dental enamel, sweat, and gastric acid.

semantic_type.name: Organism Attribute
semantic_type.definition: A property of the organism or its major parts.

semantic_type.name: Finding
semantic_type.definition: That which is discovered by direct observation or measurement of an organism attribute or condition, including the clinical history of the patient. The history of the presence of a disease is a 'Finding' and is distinguished from the disease itself.

semantic_type.name: Laboratory or Test Result
semantic_type.definition: The outcome of a specific test to measure an attribute or to determine the presence, absence, or degree of a condition.

semantic_type.name: Injury or Poisoning
semantic_type.definition: A traumatic wound, injury, or poisoning caused by an external agent or force.

semantic_type.name: Biologic Function
semantic_type.definition: A state, activity or process of the body or one of its systems or parts.

semantic_type.name: Physiologic Function
semantic_type.definition: A normal process, activity, or state of the body.

semantic_type.name: Organism Function
semantic_type.definition: A physiologic function of the organism as a whole, of multiple organ systems, or of multiple organs or tissues.

semantic_type.name: Mental Process
semantic_type.definition: A physiologic function involving the mind or cognitive processing.

semantic_type.name: Organ or Tissue Function
semantic_type.definition: A physiologic function of a particular organ, organ system, or tissue.

semantic_type.name: Cell Function
semantic_type.definition: A physiologic function inherent to cells or cell components.

semantic_type.name: Molecular Function
semantic_type.definition: A physiologic function occurring at the molecular level.

semantic_type.name: Genetic Function
semantic_type.definition: Functions of or related to the maintenance, translation or expression of the genetic material.

semantic_type.name: Pathologic Function
semantic_type.definition: A disordered process, activity, or state of the organism as a whole, of a body system or systems, or of multiple organs or tissues. Included here are normal responses to a negative stimulus as well as patholologic conditions or states that are less specific than a disease. Pathologic functions frequently have systemic effects.

semantic_type.name: Disease or Syndrome
semantic_type.definition: A condition which alters or interferes with a normal process, state, or activity of an organism. It is usually characterized by the abnormal functioning of one or more of the host's systems, parts, or organs. Included here is a complex of symptoms descriptive of a disorder.

semantic_type.name: Mental or Behavioral Dysfunction
semantic_type.definition: A clinically significant dysfunction whose major manifestation is behavioral or psychological. These dysfunctions may have identified or presumed biological etiologies or manifestations.

semantic_type.name: Cell or Molecular Dysfunction
semantic_type.definition: A pathologic function inherent to cells, parts of cells, or molecules.

semantic_type.name: Experimental Model of Disease
semantic_type.definition: A representation in a non-human organism of a human disease for the purpose of research into its mechanism or treatment.

semantic_type.name: Event
semantic_type.definition: A broad type for grouping activities, processes and states.

semantic_type.name: Activity
semantic_type.definition: An operation or series of operations that an organism or machine carries out or participates in.

semantic_type.name: Behavior
semantic_type.definition: Any of the psycho-social activities of humans or animals that can be observed directly by others or can be made systematically observable by the use of special strategies.

semantic_type.name: Social Behavior
semantic_type.definition: Behavior that is a direct result or function of the interaction of humans or animals with their fellows. This includes behavior that may be considered anti-social.

semantic_type.name: Individual Behavior
semantic_type.definition: Behavior exhibited by a human or an animal that is not a direct result of interaction with other members of the species, but which may have an effect on others.

semantic_type.name: Daily or Recreational Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity carried out for recreation or exercise, or as part of daily life.

semantic_type.name: Occupational Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity carried out as part of an occupation or job.

semantic_type.name: Health Care Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity of or relating to the practice of medicine or involving the care of patients.

semantic_type.name: Laboratory Procedure
semantic_type.definition: A procedure, method, or technique used to determine the composition, quantity, or concentration of a specimen, and which is carried out in a clinical laboratory. Included here are procedures which measure the times and rates of reactions.

semantic_type.name: Diagnostic Procedure
semantic_type.definition: A procedure, method, or technique used to determine the nature or identity of a disease or disorder. This excludes procedures which are primarily carried out on specimens in a laboratory.

semantic_type.name: Therapeutic or Preventive Procedure
semantic_type.definition: A procedure, method, or technique designed to prevent a disease or a disorder, or to improve physical function, or used in the process of treating a disease or injury.

semantic_type.name: Research Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity carried out as part of research or experimentation.

semantic_type.name: Molecular Biology Research Technique
semantic_type.definition: Any of the techniques used in the study of or the directed modification of the gene complement of a living organism.

semantic_type.name: Governmental or Regulatory Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity carried out by officially constituted governments, or an activity related to the creation or enforcement of the rules or regulations governing some field of endeavor.

semantic_type.name: Educational Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity related to the organization and provision of education.

semantic_type.name: Machine Activity
semantic_type.definition: An activity carried out primarily or exclusively by machines.

semantic_type.name: Phenomenon or Process
semantic_type.definition: A process or state which occurs naturally or as a result of an activity.

semantic_type.name: Human-caused Phenomenon or Process
semantic_type.definition: A phenomenon or process that is a result of the activities of human beings.

semantic_type.name: Environmental Effect of Humans
semantic_type.definition: A change in the natural environment that is a result of the activities of human beings.

semantic_type.name: Natural Phenomenon or Process
semantic_type.definition: A phenomenon or process that occurs irrespective of the activities of human beings.

semantic_type.name: Entity
semantic_type.definition: A broad type for grouping physical and conceptual entities.

semantic_type.name: Physical Object
semantic_type.definition: An object perceptible to the sense of vision or touch.

semantic_type.name: Manufactured Object
semantic_type.definition: A physical object made by human beings.

semantic_type.name: Medical Device
semantic_type.definition: A manufactured object used primarily in the diagnosis, treatment, or prevention of physiologic or anatomic disorders.

semantic_type.name: Research Device
semantic_type.definition: A manufactured object used primarily in carrying out scientific research or experimentation.

semantic_type.name: Conceptual Entity
semantic_type.definition: A broad type for grouping abstract entities or concepts.

semantic_type.name: Idea or Concept
semantic_type.definition: An abstract concept, such as a social, religious or philosophical concept.

semantic_type.name: Temporal Concept
semantic_type.definition: A concept which pertains to time or duration.

semantic_type.name: Qualitative Concept
semantic_type.definition: A concept which is an assessment of some quality, rather than a direct measurement.

semantic_type.name: Quantitative Concept
semantic_type.definition: A concept which involves the dimensions, quantity or capacity of something using some unit of measure, or which involves the quantitative comparison of entities.

semantic_type.name: Spatial Concept
semantic_type.definition: A location, region, or space, generally having definite boundaries.

semantic_type.name: Geographic Area
semantic_type.definition: A geographic location, generally having definite boundaries.

semantic_type.name: Molecular Sequence
semantic_type.definition: A broad type for grouping the collected sequences of amino acids, carbohydrates, and nucleotide sequences. Descriptions of these sequences are generally reported in the published literature and/or are deposited in and maintained by databanks such as GenBank, European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL), National Biomedical Research Foundation (NBRF), or other sequence repositories.

semantic_type.name: Nucleotide Sequence
semantic_type.definition: The sequence of purines and pyrimidines in nucleic acids and polynucleotides. Included here are nucleotide-rich regions, conserved sequence, and DNA transforming region.

semantic_type.name: Amino Acid Sequence
semantic_type.definition: The sequence of amino acids as arrayed in chains, sheets, etc., within the protein molecule. It is of fundamental importance in determining protein structure.

semantic_type.name: Carbohydrate Sequence
semantic_type.definition: The sequence of carbohydrates within polysaccharides, glycoproteins, and glycolipids.

semantic_type.name: Regulation or Law
semantic_type.definition: An intellectual product resulting from legislative or regulatory activity.

semantic_type.name: Occupation or Discipline
semantic_type.definition: A vocation, academic discipline, or field of study, or a subpart of an occupation or discipline.

semantic_type.name: Biomedical Occupation or Discipline
semantic_type.definition: A vocation, academic discipline, or field of study related to biomedicine.

semantic_type.name: Organization
semantic_type.definition: The result of uniting for a common purpose or function. The continued existence of an organization is not dependent on any of its members, its location, or particular facility. Components or subparts of organizations are also included here. Although the names of organizations are sometimes used to refer to the buildings in which they reside, they are not inherently physical in nature.

semantic_type.name: Health Care Related Organization
semantic_type.definition: An established organization which carries out specific functions related to health care delivery or research in the life sciences.

semantic_type.name: Professional Society
semantic_type.definition: An organization uniting those who have a common vocation or who are involved with a common field of study.

semantic_type.name: Self-help or Relief Organization
semantic_type.definition: An organization whose purpose and function is to provide assistance to the needy or to offer support to those sharing similar problems.

semantic_type.name: Group
semantic_type.definition: A conceptual entity referring to the classification of individuals according to certain shared characteristics.

semantic_type.name: Professional or Occupational Group
semantic_type.definition: An individual or individuals classified according to their vocation.

semantic_type.name: Population Group
semantic_type.definition: An indivdual or individuals classified according to their sex, racial origin, religion, common place of living, financial or social status, or some other cultural or behavioral attribute.

semantic_type.name: Family Group
semantic_type.definition: An individual or individuals classified according to their family relationships or relative position in the family unit.

semantic_type.name: Age Group
semantic_type.definition: An individual or individuals classified according to their age.

semantic_type.name: Patient or Disabled Group
semantic_type.definition: An individual or individuals classified according to a disability, disease, condition or treatment.

semantic_type.name: Group Attribute
semantic_type.definition: A conceptual entity which refers to the frequency or distribution of certain characteristics or phenomena in certain groups.

semantic_type.name: Chemical
semantic_type.definition: Compounds or substances of definite molecular composition. Chemicals are viewed from two distinct perspectives in the network, functionally and structurally. Almost every chemical concept is assigned at least two types, generally one from the structure hierarchy and at least one from the function hierarchy.

semantic_type.name: Chemical Viewed Structurally
semantic_type.definition: A chemical or chemicals viewed from the perspective of their structural characteristics. Included here are concepts which can mean either a salt, an ion, or a compound (e.g., "Bromates" and "Bromides").

semantic_type.name: Organic Chemical
semantic_type.definition: The general class of carbon-containing compounds, usually based on carbon chains or rings, and also containing hydrogen (hydrocarbons), with or without nitrogen, oxygen, or other elements in which the bonding between elements is generally covalent.

semantic_type.name: Nucleic Acid, Nucleoside, or Nucleotide
semantic_type.definition: A complex compound of high molecular weight occurring in living cells. These are basically of two types, ribonucleic (RNA) and deoxyribonucleic (DNA) acids. Nucleic acids are made of nucleotides (nitrogen-containing base, a 5-carbon sugar, and one or more phosphate group) linked together by a phosphodiester bond between the 5' and 3' carbon atoms. Nucleosides are compounds composed of a purine or pyrimidine base (usually adenine, cytosine, guanine, thymine, uracil) linked to either a ribose or a deoxyribose sugar.

semantic_type.name: Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein
semantic_type.definition: Amino acids and chains of amino acids connected by peptide linkages.

semantic_type.name: Chemical Viewed Functionally
semantic_type.definition: A chemical viewed from the perspective of its functional characteristics or pharmacological activities.

semantic_type.name: Pharmacologic Substance
semantic_type.definition: A substance used in the treatment or prevention of pathologic disorders. This includes substances that occur naturally in the body and are administered therapeutically.

semantic_type.name: Biomedical or Dental Material
semantic_type.definition: A substance used in biomedicine or dentistry predominantly for its physical, as opposed to chemical, properties. Included here are biocompatible materials, tissue adhesives, bone cements, resins, toothpastes, etc.

semantic_type.name: Biologically Active Substance
semantic_type.definition: A generally endogenous substance produced or required by an organism, of primary interest because of its role in the biologic functioning of the organism that produces it.

semantic_type.name: Hormone
semantic_type.definition: In animals, a chemical usually secreted by an endocrine gland whose products are released into the circulating fluid. Hormones act as chemical messengers and regulate various physiologic processes such as growth, reproduction, metabolism, etc. They usually fall into two broad classes, steroid hormones and peptide hormones.

semantic_type.name: Enzyme
semantic_type.definition: A complex chemical, usually a protein, that is produced by living cells and which catalyzes specific biochemical reactions. There are six main types of enzymes: oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, and ligases.

semantic_type.name: Vitamin
semantic_type.definition: A substance, usually an organic chemical complex, present in natural products or made synthetically, which is essential in the diet of man or other higher animals. Included here are vitamin precursors, provitamins, and vitamin supplements.

semantic_type.name: Immunologic Factor
semantic_type.definition: A biologically active substance whose activities affect or play a role in the functioning of the immune system.

semantic_type.name: Indicator, Reagent, or Diagnostic Aid
semantic_type.definition: A substance primarily of interest for its use in laboratory or diagnostic tests and procedures to detect, measure, examine, or analyze other chemicals, processes, or conditions.

semantic_type.name: Hazardous or Poisonous Substance
semantic_type.definition: A substance of concern because of its potentially hazardous or toxic effects. This would include most drugs of abuse, as well as agents that require special handling because of their toxicity.

semantic_type.name: Substance
semantic_type.definition: A material with definite or fairly definite chemical composition.

semantic_type.name: Food
semantic_type.definition: Any substance generally containing nutrients, such as carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, that can be ingested by a living organism and metabolized into energy and body tissue. Some foods are naturally occurring, others are either partially or entirely made by humans.

semantic_type.name: Functional Concept
semantic_type.definition: A concept which is of interest because it pertains to the carrying out of a process or activity.

semantic_type.name: Intellectual Product
semantic_type.definition: A conceptual entity resulting from human endeavor. Concepts assigned to this type generally refer to information created by humans for some purpose.

semantic_type.name: Language
semantic_type.definition: The system of communication used by a particular nation or people.

semantic_type.name: Sign or Symptom
semantic_type.definition: An observable manifestation of a disease or condition based on clinical judgment, or a manifestation of a disease or condition which is experienced by the patient and reported as a subjective observation.

semantic_type.name: Classification
semantic_type.definition: A term or system of terms denoting an arrangement by class or category.

semantic_type.name: Anatomical Abnormality
semantic_type.definition: An abnormal structure, or one that is abnormal in size or location.

semantic_type.name: Neoplastic Process
semantic_type.definition: A new and abnormal growth of tissue in which the growth is uncontrolled and progressive. The growths may be malignant or benign.

semantic_type.name: Receptor
semantic_type.definition: A specific structure or site on the cell surface or within its cytoplasm that recognizes and binds with other specific molecules. These include the proteins on the surface of an immunocompetent cell that binds with antigens, or proteins found on the surface molecules that bind with hormones or neurotransmitters and react with other molecules that respond in a specific way.

semantic_type.name: Archaeon
semantic_type.definition: A member of one of the three domains of life, formerly called Archaebacteria under the taxon Bacteria, but now considered separate and distinct. Archaea are characterized by: 1) the presence of characteristic tRNAs and ribosomal RNAs; 2) the absence of peptidoglycan cell walls; 3) the presence of ether-linked lipids built from branched-chain subunits; and 4) their occurrence in unusual habitats. While archaea resemble bacteria in morphology and genomic organization, they resemble eukarya in their method of genomic replication.

semantic_type.name: Antibiotic
semantic_type.definition: A pharmacologically active compound produced by growing microorganisms which kill or inhibit growth of other microorganisms.

semantic_type.name: Element, Ion, or Isotope
semantic_type.definition: One of the 109 presently known fundamental substances that comprise all matter at and above the atomic level. This includes elemental metals, rare gases, and most abundant naturally occurring radioactive elements, as well as the ionic counterparts of elements (NA+, Cl-), and the less abundant isotopic forms. This does not include organic ions such as iodoacetate to which the type 'Organic Chemical' is assigned.

semantic_type.name: Inorganic Chemical
semantic_type.definition: Chemical elements and their compounds, excluding the hydrocarbons and their derivatives (except carbides, carbonates, cyanides, cyanates and carbon disulfide). Generally inorganic compounds contain ionic bonds. Included here are inorganic acids and salts, alloys, alkalies, and minerals.

semantic_type.name: Clinical Drug
semantic_type.definition: A pharmaceutical preparation as produced by the manufacturer. The name usually includes the substance, its strength, and the form, but may include the substance and only one of the other two items.

semantic_type.name: Clinical Attribute
semantic_type.definition: An observable or measurable property or state of an organism of clinical interest.

semantic_type.name: Drug Delivery Device
semantic_type.definition: A medical device that contains a clinical drug or drugs.

semantic_type.name: Eukaryote
semantic_type.definition: One of the three domains of life (the others being Bacteria and Archaea), also called Eukarya. These are organisms whose cells are enclosed in membranes and possess a nucleus. They comprise almost all multicellular and many unicellular organisms, and are traditionally divided into groups (sometimes called kingdoms) including Animals, Plants, Fungi, various Algae, and other taxa that were previously part of the old kingdom Protista.

sematic_relation.name: physically_related_to
sematic_relation.definition: Related by virtue of some physical attribute or characteristic.

sematic_relation.name: part_of
sematic_relation.definition: Composes, with one or more other physical units, some larger whole. This includes component of, division of, portion of, fragment of, section of, and layer of.

sematic_relation.name: contains
sematic_relation.definition: Holds or is the receptacle for fluids or other substances. This includes is filled with, holds, and is occupied by.

sematic_relation.name: location_of
sematic_relation.definition: The position, site, or region of an entity or the site of a process.

sematic_relation.name: temporally_related_to
sematic_relation.definition: Related in time by preceding, co-occuring with, or following.

sematic_relation.name: co-occurs_with
sematic_relation.definition: Occurs at the same time as, together with, or jointly. This includes is co-incident with, is concurrent with, is contemporaneous with, accompanies, coexists with, and is concomitant with.

sematic_relation.name: precedes
sematic_relation.definition: Occurs earlier in time. This includes antedates, comes before, is in advance of, predates, and is prior to.

sematic_relation.name: functionally_related_to
sematic_relation.definition: Related by the carrying out of some function or activity.

sematic_relation.name: process_of
sematic_relation.definition: Action, function, or state of.

sematic_relation.name: carries_out
sematic_relation.definition: Executes a function or performs a procedure or activity. This includes transacts, operates on, handles, and executes.

sematic_relation.name: interacts_with
sematic_relation.definition: Acts, functions, or operates together with.

sematic_relation.name: practices
sematic_relation.definition: Performs habitually or customarily.

sematic_relation.name: produces
sematic_relation.definition: Brings forth, generates or creates. This includes yields, secretes, emits, biosynthesizes, generates, releases, discharges, and creates.

sematic_relation.name: exhibits
sematic_relation.definition: Shows or demonstrates.

sematic_relation.name: disrupts
sematic_relation.definition: Alters or influences an already existing condition, state, or situation. Produces a negative effect on.

sematic_relation.name: causes
sematic_relation.definition: Brings about a condition or an effect. Implied here is that an agent, such as for example, a pharmacologic substance or an organism, has brought about the effect. This includes induces, effects, evokes, and etiology.

sematic_relation.name: prevents
sematic_relation.definition: Stops, hinders or eliminates an action or condition.

sematic_relation.name: complicates
sematic_relation.definition: Causes to become more severe or complex or results in adverse effects.

sematic_relation.name: manifestation_of
sematic_relation.definition: That part of a phenomenon which is directly observable or concretely or visibly expressed, or which gives evidence to the underlying process. This includes expression of, display of, and exhibition of.

sematic_relation.name: affects
sematic_relation.definition: Produces a direct effect on. Implied here is the altering or influencing of an existing condition, state, situation, or entity. This includes has a role in, alters, influences, predisposes, catalyzes, stimulates, regulates, depresses, impedes, enhances, contributes to, leads to, and modifies.

sematic_relation.name: occurs_in
sematic_relation.definition: Takes place in or happens under given conditions, circumstances, or time periods, or in a given location or population. This includes appears in, transpires, comes about, is present in, and exists in.

sematic_relation.name: manages
sematic_relation.definition: Administers, or contributes to the care of an individual or group of individuals.

sematic_relation.name: treats
sematic_relation.definition: Applies a remedy with the object of effecting a cure or managing a condition.

sematic_relation.name: uses
sematic_relation.definition: Employs in the carrying out of some activity. This includes applies, utilizes, employs, and avails.

sematic_relation.name: indicates
sematic_relation.definition: Gives evidence for the presence at some time of an entity or process.

sematic_relation.name: result_of
sematic_relation.definition: The condition, product, or state occurring as a consequence, effect, or conclusion of an activity or process. This includes product of, effect of, sequel of, outcome of, culmination of, and completion of.

sematic_relation.name: conceptually_related_to
sematic_relation.definition: Related by some abstract concept, thought, or idea.

sematic_relation.name: property_of
sematic_relation.definition: Characteristic of, or quality of.

sematic_relation.name: conceptual_part_of
sematic_relation.definition: Conceptually a portion, division, or component of some larger whole.

sematic_relation.name: evaluation_of
sematic_relation.definition: Judgment of the value or degree of some attribute or process.

sematic_relation.name: measures
sematic_relation.definition: Ascertains or marks the dimensions, quantity, degree, or capacity of.

sematic_relation.name: diagnoses
sematic_relation.definition: Distinguishes or identifies the nature or characteristics of.

sematic_relation.name: assesses_effect_of
sematic_relation.definition: Analyzes the influence or consequences of the function or action of.

sematic_relation.name: issue_in
sematic_relation.definition: Is an issue in or a point of discussion, study, debate, or dispute.

sematic_relation.name: associated_with
sematic_relation.definition: has a significant or salient relationship to.

sematic_relation.name: consists_of
sematic_relation.definition: Is structurally made up of in whole or in part of some material or matter. This includes composed of, made of, and formed of.

sematic_relation.name: adjacent_to
sematic_relation.definition: Close to, near or abutting another physical unit with no other structure of the same kind intervening. This includes adjoins, abuts, is contiguous to, is juxtaposed, and is close to.

sematic_relation.name: connected_to
sematic_relation.definition: Directly attached to another physical unit as tendons are connected to muscles. This includes attached to and anchored to.

sematic_relation.name: interconnects
sematic_relation.definition: Serves to link or join together two or more other physical units. This includes joins, links, conjoins, articulates, separates, and bridges.

sematic_relation.name: surrounds
sematic_relation.definition: Establishes the boundaries for, or defines the limits of another physical structure. This includes limits, bounds, confines, encloses, and circumscribes.

sematic_relation.name: traverses
sematic_relation.definition: Crosses or extends across another physical structure or area. This includes crosses over and crosses through.

sematic_relation.name: derivative_of
sematic_relation.definition: In chemistry, a substance structurally related to another or that can be made from the other substance. This is used only for structural relationships. This does not include functional relationships such as metabolite of, by product of, nor analog of.

sematic_relation.name: developmental_form_of
sematic_relation.definition: An earlier stage in the individual maturation of.

sematic_relation.name: degree_of
sematic_relation.definition: The relative intensity of a process or the relative intensity or amount of a quality or attribute.

sematic_relation.name: measurement_of
sematic_relation.definition: The dimension, quantity, or capacity determined by measuring.

sematic_relation.name: method_of
sematic_relation.definition: The manner and sequence of events in performing an act or procedure.

sematic_relation.name: isa
sematic_relation.definition: The basic hierarchical link in the Network. If one item "isa" another item then the first item is more specific in meaning than the second item.

sematic_relation.name: brings_about
sematic_relation.definition: Acts on or influences an entity.

sematic_relation.name: performs
sematic_relation.definition: Executes, accomplishes, or achieves an activity.

sematic_relation.name: spatially_related_to
sematic_relation.definition: Related by place or region.

sematic_relation.name: analyzes
sematic_relation.definition: Studies or examines using established quantitative or qualitative methods.

sematic_relation.name: branch_of
sematic_relation.definition: Arises from the division of. For example, the arborization of arteries.

sematic_relation.name: tributary_of
sematic_relation.definition: Merges with. For example, the confluence of veins.

sematic_relation.name: ingredient_of
sematic_relation.definition: Is a component of, as in a constituent of a preparation.
